title: Agile Scaling Frameworks
summary: Recognise the major agile scaling frameworks by their core ideas and keywords, treat scaling as a tailoring decision, and avoid over-engineering multi-team work.
intro: Spot SoS, LeSS, SAFe, Nexus, and DA by their keywords - and know when not to scale at all.

sections.heading: Why and when to scale
sections.body:
- Scale when several teams share one product, one value stream, or tightly coupled deliverables - not just because the organisation is large.
- Coordination cost rises non-linearly: more handoffs, dependencies, and integration risk mean slower flow if teams stay isolated.
- On the current exam, scaling is a tailoring choice - PMBOK 8 stresses principles, performance domains, and fit-for-purpose process, not a mandate to adopt a named framework.
- Prefer the lightest structure that protects delivery: get single-team practices right first, then add cross-team coordination only when complexity truly demands it.
- Exam keywords: multi-team product, dependency management, integration risk, and do not over-scale.
sections.tip: If one backlog and informal dependency chats would work, don't invent a scaling framework.

sections.heading: Scrum of Scrums
sections.body:
- A lightweight 'team of teams' sync: each team sends a representative to surface dependencies, blockers, and integration issues.
- The focus is coordination and risk, not status theatre - representatives speak for their team and carry decisions back.
- It is often the first scaling step once a few teams share one product.
- It adds minimal new roles or artifacts and extends existing events rather than replacing them.
- Recognition cues: Scrum of Scrums, ambassadors or representatives, dependency sync, lightest-weight scaling.
sections.tip: Scrum of Scrums = simple multi-team coordination; know the idea, not a ceremony script.

sections.heading: LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum)
sections.body:
- Minimalist scaling: keep Scrum and add as little structure as possible.
- The classic pattern is one Product Owner, one Product Backlog, and a shared Definition of Done across the teams building one product.
- Teams stay feature-oriented and cross-functional; the aim is to cut overhead, not add management layers.
- Coordination comes from the shared backlog, joint refinement, and multi-team events - not heavy portfolio machinery.
- Exam cues: descaling, one backlog and one PO, pure Scrum at scale, avoid unnecessary process.
sections.tip: LeSS = the same Scrum over a wider product; 'minimal extras' is the keyword story.

sections.heading: SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework)
sections.body:
- Enterprise-oriented and the most prescriptive of the common frameworks - many roles, events, and layered structure.
- Familiar levels: Team, Program (the Agile Release Train, or ART, with PI Planning), Large Solution, and Portfolio.
- The Agile Release Train aligns multiple teams to a shared cadence, vision, and program-increment goals.
- It is best known for large organisations needing portfolio alignment, fixed planning horizons, and formal coordination.
- Exam reality: name the levels, the ART, and PI Planning; deep role catalogues are rarely tested.
sections.tip: SAFe = heavyweight, ART + PI Planning, the most rules and hierarchy of the popular options.

sections.heading: Nexus and Disciplined Agile
sections.body:
- Nexus builds on Scrum for roughly 3 to 9 teams delivering one product; a Nexus Integration Team owns cross-team integration and dependency risk.
- It keeps a single Product Owner and backlog focus while adding integration-centric events and artifacts.
- Disciplined Agile (DA) is a toolkit and decision framework rather than one fixed method - it helps a team choose its Way of Working (WoW) from many practices.
- DA is pragmatic and hybrid-friendly: it guides choices instead of prescribing one recipe for the whole organisation.
- Exam cues: Nexus = integration team + Scrum at scale; DA = choose your WoW, a process-decision toolkit.
sections.tip: Match the keyword - Nexus 'integration', DA 'WoW / toolkit' - and stop there unless the question digs deeper.

sections.heading: The frameworks at a glance
sections.body: Use this as a recognition map - match the scenario's size, formality, and integration pain to the framework name. The best choice still sits under tailoring: the lightest fit that solves the coordination problem.

items.text: Representatives sync dependencies; few teams, early scaling; low ceremony.
One PO, one backlog, shared Definition of Done; one product, several Scrum teams; low to moderate.
Scrum plus an integration team; about 3 to 9 teams on one product; moderate.
Enterprise trains and PI cadence; large org, portfolio alignment; highly prescriptive.
Choose a fit-for-purpose Way of Working; varied or hybrid contexts; a flexible toolkit.

sections.tip: The exam rewards recognition - name, core idea, and weight - not framework-certification depth.

sections.heading: Scaling pitfalls and the PM takeaway
sections.body:
- Too much process too early: adopting SAFe-level structure when Scrum of Scrums or basic backlog hygiene would do kills speed and morale.
- Lost team autonomy: over-centralising decisions recreates waterfall under an agile label ('faux-agile').
- Dependency gridlock: many teams, unclear ownership, and late integration - counter it with a shared Definition of Done, frequent integration, and explicit dependency management.
- Framework worship: copying roles and events without value delivery fails performance-domain thinking.
- PM takeaway: scale only when multi-team complexity demands it, and tailor coordination to risk, size, and hybrid reality - not to a brand name.
sections.tip: Prefer the smallest scaling step that restores flow; 'start simple' is a valid PMP answer.

source: PM Cram - aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
